quotes.text: All beings desire happiness; therefore to all extend your benevolence.
Because he has pity upon every living creature, therefore is a man called ‘holy.’
Like as a mother at the risk of her life watches over her…only child, so also let every one cultivate towards all beings a boundless (friendly) mind.
Hurt not others with that which pains yourself.
Identifying himself with others.
The king is to us even as a father; he loves us even as he loves himself; we are to the king even as (his) children.
quotes.source: Rock inscriptions of Asoka (Separate edicts. no. 2)

quotes.text: With pure thoughts and fullness of love, I will do to others what I do for myself.
He lives only to be a help to others.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch.2, sec. 30)

quotes.text: Why should we cling to this perishable body? In the eye of the wise, the only thing it is good for is to benefit one’s fellow-creatures.
Is not all I possess, even to my very body, kept for the benefit of others?
Forbearance was our Buddha wont to teach.
though a man with a sharp sword should cut one’s body bit by bit, let not an angry thought…arise, let the mouth speak no ill word.
Them who became thy murderers, thou forgavest.
Overcome evil by good.
Conquer your foe by force, and you increase his enmity; conquer by love, and you reap no after-sorrow.
This great principle of returning good for evil.
The member of Buddha’s order…should not intentionally destroy the life of any being, down even to a worm or an ant.

Whether now any man kill with his own hand, or command any other to kill, or whether he only see with pleasure that act of killing - all is equally forbidden by this law, and many other things which cannot be described one by one.

My teaching is this, that the slightest act of charity, even in the lowest class of persons, such as saving the life of an insect out of pity, that this act… shall bring to the doer of it consequent benefit.
He came to remove the sorrows of all living things.
He who holds up a torch to (lighten) mankind is always honoured by me.
Aims so high, and endeavours so grand.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 1, sec. 41)

quotes.text: Now (said he) I will seek a noble law, unlike the worldly methods known to men,... and will fight against the mischief wrought upon man by sickness, age, and death.
The prince has grown up in a palace, with every care bestowed upon his tender person; and now he gives his body to the… thorny forest: how shall he bear a life of privation?
That you who of right might rule the earth,... should now go begging here and there your food! … not receiving the tribute of the world, but begging food sufficient for … nourishment!

Thou flower of all thy race! Confessedly the most renowned! … ever reverenced without self-seeking! … thou who wert wont to repose upon a soft and kingly couch! … how canst thou endure the mountain and the forest wilds, on the bare grass to make thyself a resting-place?
All men should cultivate a fixed and firm determination, and vow that what they once undertake they will never give up.
May my body be ground to powder small as the mustard-seed if I ever desire to (break my vow)!
This, though I gained heave’n high state cannot be done! How much less to gain an earthly … home!
Rather will I fall headlong into this hell, … than do a deed that is unworthy.
Happy is he that is virtuous.
To make an end of selfishness is happiness.
There is no happiness except in righteousness.
Full of love for all things in the world, practising virtue in order to benefit others — this man only is happy.
He that loveth iniquity, beckoneth to misfortune.
